policy_statement: Conduct research on emerging forest products and markets related to bioeconomy and harvested wood product initiatives.
mechanism_description: Conducting research on emerging forest products and bioeconomy markets supports harvested wood product initiatives that can increase long-lived carbon storage in wood products and substitute for more emissions-intensive materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Forest product innovation/market development → ↑ long-lived harvested wood product use → (↑ carbon stored in product pools + ↓ high-embodied-carbon material demand) → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Public/industry R&D and market development accelerates commercialization and adoption of harvested wood products (e.g., mass timber, engineered wood) and associated bioeconomy supply chains.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Carbon sequestered in forests is transferred into durable product pools (delaying oxidation), while construction/manufacturing shifts from steel/cement and other emissions-intensive materials to wood, reducing process and energy emissions across the lifecycle.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions via (i) increased carbon storage in long-lived wood products and (ii) avoided emissions from material substitution.
dominant_pathway_test: Increasing the supply and use of long-lived wood products leads to net lifecycle GHG reductions through carbon stored in products and substitution away from more emissions-intensive materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced net GHG emissions through harvested wood product carbon storage and substitution benefits.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it concerns “wood” and forests (often linked to Nature-Based Solutions) and can improve material efficiency (Resource Efficiency), the primary climate claim is emissions mitigation via product carbon pools and avoided embodied emissions, not hazard reduction (Adaptation) or ecosystem enhancement as the main lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: If policies under this mechanism were instead primarily about forest management for standing-stock sequestration/biodiversity, they could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; however, the stated mechanism is explicitly about harvested wood products and markets, which primarily operate through lifecycle emissions mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy framed as research/market development for harvested wood products intending carbon storage/substitution has its principal climate impact through mitigation of lifecycle emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More specific subtypes (e.g., “materials substitution,” “product carbon storage,” “R&D enabling action”) require policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Outcomes depend on forest carbon accounting, sustainable harvest/regrowth, product half-life, end-of-life treatment, and leakage; if harvesting increases net forest carbon losses or short-lived products dominate, benefits may be reduced. Research-only actions are enabling and may have uncertain/indirect realized mitigation.
key_indicators: “Conduct research”; “work with partners”; “emerging forest products and markets”; “bioeconomy”; “harvested wood product initiatives”
co_benefits: Economic development/job creation in forestry and wood products; rural/community development; innovation competitiveness
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be coded as an incentive-program if the underlying implementation is specifically via R&D grants/funding; however, the text only commits to conducting research (no funding mechanism or requirements specified), so a planning/strategy (enabling) classification fits best.
